topic: An athlete is asked to switch positions
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coach’s voice, usually a steady anchor, sounded brittle as he laid out the new formation. "You’ll be playing center. It’s a change of pace, but you’re built for it." I stared at the whiteboard, the lines of the diagram swimming before my eyes. My hands, gripping the edge of the bench, were slick with a cold sweat. Every muscle in my body felt suddenly heavy, unresponsive. I tried to nod, but my throat was tight, refusing to cooperate. The air in the locker room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my chest. When the coach looked my way, his expression was expectant, and that simple gaze felt like an interrogation. I just wanted the floor to swallow me whole.

[story 2]

"Just try it," the manager urged, clapping me lightly on the shoulder. "It’s just a few drills. You can adjust." I shook my head, a slow, jerky motion. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I watched the other players begin to move into their new spots, fluid and confident. My legs felt disconnected from my mind, as if I were piloting a machine that had suddenly lost its controls. I focused intensely on the scuff marks on the floor, counting them, trying to anchor myself to something solid. Inside, a frantic buzzing started behind my eyes, a high-pitched whine that drowned out everything else. I mumbled something incoherent, unable to meet anyone’s gaze.

[story 3]

The announcement echoed in the quiet tunnel, a sharp, unwelcome sound. When the coach approached, his tone was measured, professional, utterly devoid of the usual camaraderie. "We need you at the pivot position for the next match." I felt a strange hollowness bloom in my stomach. My knuckles were white where I gripped my water bottle. I looked down at my shoes, tracing the worn leather with my fingertip, unable to look up. The world seemed to tilt slightly, colors blurring at the edges. A wave of intense pressure built behind my sternum, making it hard to draw a full breath. I managed a barely audible, "Okay," the sound scraping out of my chest like gravel.
